original_abstract:
To characterize the impact of agricultural waste combustion on the free troposphere, we analyzed aerosol (TSP) samples collected at the summit of Mount Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) in the North China Plain. Sampling was conducted from 29 May to 28 June 2006, covering both the peak wheat straw burning season and the subsequent post-burning period. Using capillary gas chromatography and GC-MS, we quantified low-molecular-weight dicarboxylic acids, ketoacids, and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls.

Our analysis revealed exceptionally high concentrations of dicarboxylic acids (C2–C11, ranging from 220 to 6070 ng m⁻³), dominated by oxalic acid (C2), followed by succinic (C4) or malonic (C3) acids. We also identified unsaturated aliphatic diacids (maleic, isomaleic, and fumaric) and aromatic diacids (phthalic, isophthalic, and terephthalic). $\omega$-Oxocarboxylic acids were the second most abundant class, led by glyoxylic acid, while pyruvic acid and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls (glyoxal and methylglyoxal) were also present in significant quantities. Remarkably, these mountain-top diacid levels exceeded those typically reported at ground level in Chinese megacities by several-fold.

Temporal trends showed that organic acid concentrations climbed steadily from late May, peaked on 7 June, and plummeted between 8–11 June as meteorological conditions shifted and wind directions changed from southerly to northerly. These variations aligned closely with total carbon (TC) and water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) levels. The data suggest that these water-soluble organics originate from a combination of direct emissions from wheat straw burning and the subsequent photochemical oxidation of volatile precursors. Other contributing mechanisms likely include the dark ozonolysis of volatile organic compounds, accretion reactions, and the oxidation of nonvolatile precursors like unsaturated fatty acids. This study underscores the profound influence that seasonal agricultural fires exert on air quality and organic chemistry within the free troposphere over Northern China.
humanized_abstract:
To investigate the influence of agricultural waste burning on free troposphere, aerosol (TSP) samples were collected at the summit of Mount Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) located in the North China Plain. The sampling campaign was performed from 29 May to 28 June 2006, covering the peak wheat straw burning period and the period after burning. Low-molecular-weight dicarboxylic acids, ketoacids and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls were identified and quantified by capillary gas chromatography and GC-MS.

Dissolved dicarboxylic acids (DCAs) in snowfall were measured for the first time at a mountain site in northern Tibetan Plateau. Our results showed concentrations of C2–C11 DCAs ranging from 220 to 6070 ng m⁻³, with oxalic acid (C2) being the major component, while concentrations of succinic (C4) and/or malonic (C3) acids were second in abundance. Other compounds such as unsaturated aliphatic diacids (maleic, isomaleic, and fumaric acids) and aromatic diacids (phthalic, isophthalic and terephthalic acids) were also detected. $\omega$-Oxocarboxylic acids were the second most abundant class of compounds, in which glyoxylic acid was found to be the dominant component with smaller amounts of pyruvic acid and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls (glyoxal and methylglyoxal). Compared with the concentrations of DCAs at the ground level in Chinese megacities, the mountain-top data here are several-fold larger.

Temporal evolution of organic acids and their source apportionment Temporal trends of organic acids for this site showed an increasing trend from late May followed by a maximum on 7 June and a falling-off trend between 8–11 June attributed to changes in the weather conditions and the dominant wind direction, which switched from southerly to northerly. TC and WSOC also exhibited strong correlation to the OA. Source apportionment revealed that water-soluble organics in the atmosphere in summer are primarily derived from wheat straw burning, accompanied by photochemical oxidation of volatile volatile volatile precursors such as aldehydes, and secondary processes involving dark ozonolysis, accretion, as well as oxidation of nonvolatile unsaturated fatty acids. The significant impact of seasonal agricultural fires in the free troposphere over Northern China on air quality and atmospheric organic chemistry is demonstrated.

document.input:
To characterize the impact of agricultural waste combustion on the free troposphere, we analyzed aerosol (TSP) samples collected at the summit of Mount Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) in the North China Plain. Sampling was conducted from 29 May to 28 June 2006, covering both the peak wheat straw burning season and the subsequent post-burning period. Using capillary gas chromatography and GC-MS, we quantified low-molecular-weight dicarboxylic acids, ketoacids, and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls.

Our analysis revealed exceptionally high concentrations of dicarboxylic acids (C2–C11, ranging from 220 to 6070 ng m⁻³), dominated by oxalic acid (C2), followed by succinic (C4) or malonic (C3) acids. We also identified unsaturated aliphatic diacids (maleic, isomaleic, and fumaric) and aromatic diacids (phthalic, isophthalic, and terephthalic). $\omega$-Oxocarboxylic acids were the second most abundant class, led by glyoxylic acid, while pyruvic acid and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls (glyoxal and methylglyoxal) were also present in significant quantities. Remarkably, these mountain-top diacid levels exceeded those typically reported at ground level in Chinese megacities by several-fold.

Temporal trends showed that organic acid concentrations climbed steadily from late May, peaked on 7 June, and plummeted between 8–11 June as meteorological conditions shifted and wind directions changed from southerly to northerly. These variations aligned closely with total carbon (TC) and water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) levels. The data suggest that these water-soluble organics originate from a combination of direct emissions from wheat straw burning and the subsequent photochemical oxidation of volatile precursors. Other contributing mechanisms likely include the dark ozonolysis of volatile organic compounds, accretion reactions, and the oxidation of nonvolatile precursors like unsaturated fatty acids. This study underscores the profound influence that seasonal agricultural fires exert on air quality and organic chemistry within the free troposphere over Northern China.
